title: Coconut wax candle with bergamot and lime
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle with coconut wax and a bright, citrus-forward essential oil blend
excerpt: Coconut wax burns cleanly at a lower temperature than soy and has a naturally creamy surface. This 200 ml jar candle uses bergamot and lime essential oils at a combined 8% of wax weight to give a bright, fresh citrus scent. Coconut wax pours at a lower temperature than soy, which means the essential oils are added and poured at 50 to 55°C rather than 60°C.
sourceNotes: Coconut wax candle making: coconut wax (76-degree refined coconut oil-based wax) melts at approximately 45 to 50°C; pour temperature 50 to 55°C. Fragrance load: 6 to 10% typical for coconut wax; 8% used here. Wick sizing: coconut wax burns with a larger melt pool at lower viscosity than soy; CD-12 suitable for a 6 to 7 cm jar. Bergamot EO (Citrus bergamia): phototoxic on skin; safe in a candle. Lime EO (Citrus aurantifolia, cold-pressed peel): phototoxic on skin; safe in a candle. Both toxic to cats.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (130 g coconut wax fill)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale weights by the number of jars. One pre-tabbed wick per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning. Coconut wax gives a very clean, smooth surface as it cools.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Wax stable for 5 years or more. Citrus top notes fade faster than base notes; the bergamot-lime scent may noticeably fade after 18 months. Keep the lid on between burns.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which liquid wax is poured into the container. Coconut wax is poured at a lower temperature than soy: 50 to 55°C. Pouring too hot causes the wax surface to ripple as it cools.

The first burn of a candle sets the wax memory: the melt pool diameter on the first burn becomes the maximum it will reach on subsequent burns. Burning to the full jar diameter on the first burn prevents tunnelling.

content.text: Coconut wax is refined from coconut oil and has a lower melting point than soy or paraffin. It burns cleanly, tends to give a very smooth, creamy-coloured surface, and has a good fragrance throw at lower oil loads. The key difference in the method is the

: coconut wax is poured at 50 to 55°C rather than the 60°C used for soy, which also means the essential oils are added at a lower temperature. Bergamot and lime are both citrus top notes that give a bright, clean scent. Both are phototoxic on skin but pose no risk in a candle; both are toxic to cats.

items.prepNote: Refined coconut wax for containers (not fractionated coconut oil); 76-degree grade
4.2% of wax weight; citrus-floral top note; phototoxic on skin, safe in a candle
3.8% of wax weight; bright citrus top note; toxic to cats
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Place the coconut wax in the candle pouring pitcher and set in a pan of barely simmering water. Coconut wax melts at a low temperature; heat gently to 55 to 60°C, stirring until fully liquid. Remove from the heat and allow to cool.
While the wax cools, secure the sustainer tab to the centre of the jar base with a dab of melted wax or a wick sticker. Thread the wick through the centring bar and rest the bar across the jar rim.
Add the essential oils and pour

When the coconut wax has cooled to 53°C, add the bergamot and lime essential oils and stir for 2 minutes. Pour immediately: coconut wax sets faster than soy and the window is shorter. Pour at pour temperature: 50 to 55°C. Pour in a slow stream down the side of the jar, leaving a 5 to 10 mm gap at the top. Leave undisturbed.
Leave for at least 1 day before the first burn. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn. The
matters for coconut wax as much as soy: burn until the melt pool reaches the full diameter of the jar, 3 to 4 hours for a 200 ml jar.

Keep the lid on between burns. Wax stable for 5 years or more; citrus top notes fade faster than base notes and may be noticeably weaker after 18 months. Store in a cool, dark place. Toxic to cats; do not burn in rooms where cats are present.
